steps.title: Inference for categories
steps.body: When data are counts in categories, chi-square tests compare what you observed to what you'd expect. This chapter covers goodness-of-fit, tests of independence, expected counts, degrees of freedom, and interpreting the chi-square statistic.

steps.title: Which chi-square test?
steps.context: A survey cross-tabulates favorite genre (3 levels) against age group (4 levels) to see if they are related.
hints: Are you comparing one variable to a fixed distribution, or two variables to each other?
Two categorical variables in a table suggests independence.

feedback.wrong: Two categorical variables in a contingency table calls for a test of independence.

solution.body: Two categorical variables in a table use the chi-square test of independence.
solution.takeaway: Goodness-of-fit checks one variable; independence checks two.

steps.title: Expected count (goodness-of-fit)
steps.context: A die is rolled 120 times. Under a fair-die null, each face is equally likely.
steps.prompt: What is the expected count for any single face?
hints: A fair die has probability 1/6 per face.
Expected count = n x probability = 120 x (1/6).

feedback.wrong: Multiply the total by each category's null probability.

solution.takeaway: Expected counts come from total sample size times null probabilities.

steps.title: Expected count (independence)
steps.context: In a contingency table the row total is 60, the column total is 50, and the grand total is 200.
steps.prompt: Compute the expected cell count: (row total x column total) / grand total.
hints: Expected = (row total x column total) / grand total.

feedback.wrong: Use (row total x column total)/grand total for each cell.

solution.takeaway: Independence expected counts assume row and column proportions are unrelated.

steps.title: One cell's contribution
steps.context: A cell has observed count O = 25 and expected count E = 20.
steps.prompt: Compute this cell's chi-square contribution: (O − E)^2 / E.

feedback.wrong: Square the difference, then divide by the expected count.

solution.takeaway: The chi-square statistic sums (O − E)^2/E over all cells.

steps.title: Degrees of freedom (table)
steps.context: A contingency table has 3 rows and 4 columns.

solution.takeaway: Independence df depends on the table's shape, not the sample size.

steps.title: Interpreting chi-square
steps.context: A goodness-of-fit test gives chi-square = 14.1 with df = 5; the 0.05 critical value is about 11.07.

options.label: Reject H0: observed counts differ from expected
Fail to reject H0: data fit the model
Accept H0: the model is exactly correct

hints: Reject when the statistic exceeds the critical value.

feedback.wrong: A statistic above the critical value leads to rejecting H0.

solution.body: Since 14.1 > 11.07, reject H0: the observed distribution differs from the expected one.
solution.takeaway: Large chi-square values mean observed and expected counts diverge.

steps.title: Direction of chi-square
steps.context: Someone asks whether chi-square tests can be one- or two-sided.
steps.prompt: Which statement is true about the chi-square test?

options.label: It is inherently right-tailed: only large values give evidence against H0
It is two-sided like a z-test

hints: Chi-square sums squared deviations, so it is never negative.
Evidence against H0 shows up as a large statistic.

feedback.wrong: Only large chi-square values count as evidence against H0.

solution.body: Because it sums squared deviations, chi-square is right-tailed; only large values reject H0.
solution.takeaway: Chi-square evidence is always in the upper tail.

steps.title: Capstone — expected counts
steps.context: A goodness-of-fit test checks if 4 product colors sell equally. Total sales n = 400.
steps.prompt: Under equal preference, what is the expected count per color?
hints: Equal preference means probability 1/4 per color.

solution.takeaway: Capstone step 1: build expected counts from the null model.

steps.title: Capstone — degrees of freedom
steps.context: There are 4 color categories in this goodness-of-fit test.

feedback.wrong: For goodness-of-fit, df is the number of categories minus one.

steps.title: Capstone — conclusion
steps.context: The test gives chi-square = 9.5 with df = 3; the 0.05 critical value is about 7.81.
steps.prompt: What do you conclude about equal color preference?

options.label: Reject H0: colors are not sold in equal proportions
Fail to reject H0: colors sell equally
Accept H0: preferences are exactly equal

hints: State the conclusion about color preference.

feedback.wrong: Since 9.5 > 7.81, reject H0 and report unequal preference.

solution.body: Because 9.5 > 7.81, reject H0: there is evidence the colors do not sell in equal proportions.
solution.takeaway: Capstone flow: expected counts, df, statistic, then a categorical conclusion.

steps.title: Chapter 9 complete
steps.body:
You can now choose between goodness-of-fit and independence tests, compute expected counts and degrees of freedom, and read the right-tailed chi-square statistic.

Next you'll bring inference to the regression line.
